request.question: Where is the official residence of the head of state of Germany?

title: Genadendal Residence
Government House, Jersey
Eastcliff (mansion)
Government House, Bermuda
President of Germany
State House (Kenya)
Politics of Berlin
Palais Schaumburg
Presidential palace
Hammerschmidt Villa
sentences: Genadendal Residence ] is the official residence of the President of South Africa when in office at Tuynhuys, Cape Town.
Genadendal is situated in Cape Town, Western Cape, South Africa.
It has been the official residence since 1994 when Nelson Mandela took up residence here rather than neighbouring Groote Schuur.
The building, formerly known as Westbrook, is named after the town of Genadendal – itself an Afrikaans word meaning "Valley of Mercy".
Government House is the official residence of the Lieutenant Governor of Jersey.
The building is situated in the parish of St Saviour in Jersey.
It is also used for ceremonial functions, receptions and meetings with visiting foreign dignitaries and heads of state.
It is also the official residence of the Duke of Normandy (currently Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom) as head of state when staying in Jersey.
Eastcliff is a 20-room house overlooking the Mississippi River in St. Paul, Minnesota, which serves as the official residence of the president of the University of Minnesota system.

It was first built in 1922 by local lumber magnate Edward Brooks Sr. and donated to the university by the Brooks family in 1958, beginning its service as the president's official residence in 1960 when O. Meredith Wilson took the position.
In 2000, the home was added to the National Register of Historic Places.
Today, it is the most-visited public residence in the state (outpacing the Minnesota Governor's Residence), often hosting events five days a week.
6,800 people were recorded attending 159 events in 2005.
Government House is the official residence of the Governor of Bermuda.
It is located on Langton Hill, overlooking the North Shore in Hamilton, Pembroke.
Government House is also the official residence of the Bermudian head of state (currently Queen Elizabeth II) when staying in Bermuda.
The President of Germany, officially the President of the Federal Republic of Germany (German: "Bundespräsident der Bundesrepublik Deutschland" ), is the head of state of Germany.
State House is the official residence of the President of Kenya.
It was the residence of the Prime Minister of Kenya from independence until Kenya (12 Dec 1963) transformed into a republic (12 Dec 1964).
As the Prime Minister's position was abolished, it has been the official residence of the president since then.
Berlin is a city-state and the capital of the Federal Republic of Germany.
The President of Germany, whose functions are primarily ceremonial under the German constitution, has his official residence in Schloss Bellevue.
Berlin is the seat of the German executive, housed in the Chancellery (the "Bundeskanzleramt").
Facing the Chancellery is the Bundestag, the German Parliament, housed in the renovated Reichstag building since the government's move to Berlin in 1998.
The Bundesrat ("federal council", functioning as an upper house) represents the Federal States ("Bundesländer") of Germany and has its seat at the former Prussian House of Lords.

Palais Schaumburg is a neoclassical-style building in Bonn, Germany, which served as the primary official seat of the German Federal Chancellery and the primary official residence of the Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1949 until 1999.
As the headquarters of the Federal Chancellery, it was simply known as "the House of the Federal Chancellor" (German: "Haus des Bundeskanzlers" ).
Since 1999, Palais Schaumburg has served as the secondary official seat of the German Federal Chancellery and the secondary official residence of the Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany.
A presidential palace is the official residence of the president in some countries.
However, some countries do not call the official residence of their head of state a "palace" or use the building only as a workplace separate from the president's actual home.
Some presidential palaces were once the official residences to monarchs in former monarchies that were preserved during those states' transition into republics.
The Cumhurbaşkanlığı Sarayı in the Turkey is the world's largest Presidential Palace, four times that of Versailles.

Hammerschmidt Villa (German: "Villa Hammerschmidt" ) is a villa located in the German city of Bonn that served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1950 until 1994.
President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994.
Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the President.
In German, the Villa is also called the "White House of Bonn", because of its vague resemblance to the official residence of the President of the United States.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

sentences: Genadendal Residence ] is the official residence of the President of South Africa when in office at Tuynhuys, Cape Town.
Genadendal is situated in Cape Town, Western Cape, South Africa.
It has been the official residence since 1994 when Nelson Mandela took up residence here rather than neighbouring Groote Schuur.
The building, formerly known as Westbrook, is named after the town of Genadendal – itself an Afrikaans word meaning "Valley of Mercy".
Government House is the official residence of the Lieutenant Governor of Jersey.
The building is situated in the parish of St Saviour in Jersey.
It is also used for ceremonial functions, receptions and meetings with visiting foreign dignitaries and heads of state.
It is also the official residence of the Duke of Normandy (currently Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom) as head of state when staying in Jersey.
Eastcliff is a 20-room house overlooking the Mississippi River in St. Paul, Minnesota, which serves as the official residence of the president of the University of Minnesota system.

It was first built in 1922 by local lumber magnate Edward Brooks Sr. and donated to the university by the Brooks family in 1958, beginning its service as the president's official residence in 1960 when O. Meredith Wilson took the position.
In 2000, the home was added to the National Register of Historic Places.
Today, it is the most-visited public residence in the state (outpacing the Minnesota Governor's Residence), often hosting events five days a week.

Government House is the official residence of the Governor of Bermuda.
It is located on Langton Hill, overlooking the North Shore in Hamilton, Pembroke.
Government House is also the official residence of the Bermudian head of state (currently Queen Elizabeth II) when staying in Bermuda.
The President of Germany, officially the President of the Federal Republic of Germany (German: "Bundespräsident der Bundesrepublik Deutschland" ), is the head of state of Germany.
State House is the official residence of the President of Kenya.
It was the residence of the Prime Minister of Kenya from independence until Kenya (12 Dec 1963) transformed into a republic (12 Dec 1964).
As the Prime Minister's position was abolished, it has been the official residence of the president since then.
Berlin is a city-state and the capital of the Federal Republic of Germany.
The President of Germany, whose functions are primarily ceremonial under the German constitution, has his official residence in Schloss Bellevue.
Berlin is the seat of the German executive, housed in the Chancellery (the "Bundeskanzleramt").
Facing the Chancellery is the Bundestag, the German Parliament, housed in the renovated Reichstag building since the government's move to Berlin in 1998.
The Bundesrat ("federal council", functioning as an upper house) represents the Federal States ("Bundesländer") of Germany and has its seat at the former Prussian House of Lords.

Palais Schaumburg is a neoclassical-style building in Bonn, Germany, which served as the primary official seat of the German Federal Chancellery and the primary official residence of the Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1949 until 1999.
As the headquarters of the Federal Chancellery, it was simply known as "the House of the Federal Chancellor" (German: "Haus des Bundeskanzlers" ).
Since 1999, Palais Schaumburg has served as the secondary official seat of the German Federal Chancellery and the secondary official residence of the Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany.
A presidential palace is the official residence of the president in some countries.
However, some countries do not call the official residence of their head of state a "palace" or use the building only as a workplace separate from the president's actual home.
Some presidential palaces were once the official residences to monarchs in former monarchies that were preserved during those states' transition into republics.
The Cumhurbaşkanlığı Sarayı in the Turkey is the world's largest Presidential Palace, four times that of Versailles.

Hammerschmidt Villa (German: "Villa Hammerschmidt" ) is a villa located in the German city of Bonn that served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1950 until 1994.
President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994.
Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the President.
In German, the Villa is also called the "White House of Bonn", because of its vague resemblance to the official residence of the President of the United States.

response.hop1_query: Where is the official residence of the head of state of Germany?
hop1_docs: Official residence | An official residence is the residence at which a nation's head of state, head of government, governor or other senior figure officially resides.  It may or may not be the same location where the individual conducts work-related functions or lives.

Royal Palace of Brussels | The Royal Palace of Brussels (Dutch: "Koninklijk Paleis van Brussel" ] , French: "Palais Royal de Bruxelles" , German: "Königlicher Palast von Brüssel" ) is the official palace of the King and Queen of the Belgians in the centre of the nation's capital Brussels.  However it is not used as a royal residence, as the king and his family live in the Royal Palace of Laeken on the outskirts of Brussels.  The website of the Belgian Monarchy describes the function of the palace as follows: ""The Palace is where His Majesty the King exercises his prerogatives as Head of State, grants audiences and deals with affairs of state.  Apart from the offices of the King and the Queen, the Royal Palace houses the services of the Grand Marshal of the Court, the King's Head of Cabinet, the Head of the King's Military Household and the Intendant of the King's Civil List.  The Palace also includes the State Rooms where large receptions are held, as well as the apartments provided for foreign Heads of State during official visits.""

Government House, Jersey | Government House is the official residence of the Lieutenant Governor of Jersey.  The building is situated in the parish of St Saviour in Jersey.  It is also used for ceremonial functions, receptions and meetings with visiting foreign dignitaries and heads of state.  It is also the official residence of the Duke of Normandy (currently Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom) as head of state when staying in Jersey.

Presidential palace | A presidential palace is the official residence of the president in some countries.  However, some countries do not call the official residence of their head of state a "palace" or use the building only as a workplace separate from the president's actual home.  Some presidential palaces were once the official residences to monarchs in former monarchies that were preserved during those states' transition into republics.  The Cumhurbaşkanlığı Sarayı in the Turkey is the world's largest Presidential Palace, four times that of Versailles.

Hammerschmidt Villa | Hammerschmidt Villa (German: "Villa Hammerschmidt" ) is a villa located in the German city of Bonn that served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1950 until 1994.  President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994.  Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the President.  In German, the Villa is also called the "White House of Bonn", because of its vague resemblance to the official residence of the President of the United States.
The Residency, Penang | The Residency in George Town, Penang, now formally named Seri Mutiara, is the official residence of the "Yang di-Pertua Negeri" (The Head of The State) of Penang.

Grand Ducal Palace, Luxembourg | The Grand Ducal Palace (Luxembourgish: "Groussherzogleche Palais" , French: "Palais grand-ducal" , German: "Großherzogliches Palais" ) is a palace in Luxembourg City, in southern Luxembourg.  It is the official residence of the Grand Duke of Luxembourg, and where he performs most of his duties as head of state of the Grand Duchy.
response.summary_1: Passage 5: "Hammerschmidt Villa ... served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany" and "President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence." Bridge entity: Bellevue Palace (Schloss Bellevue) — retrieve the Bellevue Palace or "President of Germany/Bundespräsident" page to confirm that the President is Germany's head of state and that Bellevue Palace in Berlin is the official residence of Germany's head of state. Use anchors: search for the phrases "Bellevue Palace in Berlin ... primary official seat and residence" and "President of the Federal Republic of Germany" (or "Bundespräsident head of state") on those pages.
response.hop2_query: Bellevue Palace Berlin official residence President of Germany head of state
hop2_docs: Bellevue Palace (Germany) | Bellevue Palace (German: "Schloss Bellevue" ), located in Berlin's Tiergarten district, has been the official residence of the President of Germany since 1994.  It is situated on the banks of the Spree river, near the Berlin Victory Column, along the northern edge of the Großer Tiergarten park.  Its namethe French for "beautiful view"derives from its scenic prospect over the Spree's course.

Berlin Bellevue station | Bellevue is a railway station on the Berlin Stadtbahn in the Hansaviertel district of Berlin, Germany.  It is located on the elevated Stadtbahn line and served by Berlin S-Bahn trains.  The station is named after nearby Bellevue Palace, the residence of the President of Germany.

Hammerschmidt Villa | Hammerschmidt Villa (German: "Villa Hammerschmidt" ) is a villa located in the German city of Bonn that served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1950 until 1994.  President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994.  Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the President.  In German, the Villa is also called the "White House of Bonn", because of its vague resemblance to the official residence of the President of the United States.

Capital of Germany | The capital of Germany is the city state of Berlin.  It is the seat of the President of Germany, whose official residence is Schloss Bellevue.  The Bundesrat ("federal council") is the representation of the Federal States ("Bundesländer") of Germany and has its seat at the former Prussian Herrenhaus (House of Lords).  Though most of the ministries are seated in Berlin, some of them, as well as some minor departments, are seated in Bonn, the former capital of West Germany.

Politics of Berlin | Berlin is a city-state and the capital of the Federal Republic of Germany.  The President of Germany, whose functions are primarily ceremonial under the German constitution, has his official residence in Schloss Bellevue.  Berlin is the seat of the German executive, housed in the Chancellery (the "Bundeskanzleramt").  Facing the Chancellery is the Bundestag, the German Parliament, housed in the renovated Reichstag building since the government's move to Berlin in 1998.  The Bundesrat ("federal council", functioning as an upper house) represents the Federal States ("Bundesländer") of Germany and has its seat at the former Prussian House of Lords.

Presidential palace | A presidential palace is the official residence of the president in some countries.  However, some countries do not call the official residence of their head of state a "palace" or use the building only as a workplace separate from the president's actual home.  Some presidential palaces were once the official residences to monarchs in former monarchies that were preserved during those states' transition into republics.  The Cumhurbaşkanlığı Sarayı in the Turkey is the world's largest Presidential Palace, four times that of Versailles.

Presidential Palace, Naypyidaw | The Presidential Palace (Burmese: သမ္မတအိမ်တော် ) is the executive office and official residence of the Myanmar head of state and government, the President of Myanmar, and located in the capital city of Naypyidaw.  The 100-room palace is a complex of buildings, surrounded by a moat that can be crossed by bridges.
response.summary_2: The head of state of Germany (the President/Bundespräsident) has the official residence at Bellevue Palace (Schloss Bellevue) in Berlin’s Tiergarten; sources note Bellevue has served as the President’s official residence since 1994.
response.answer: Bellevue Palace (Schloss Bellevue) in Berlin’s Tiergarten
